question: What is the primary goal of shifting security 'left' in the software development lifecycle?

options.a: To reduce the cost of marketing software products globally.
options.b: To prevent vulnerabilities by addressing them early in the process.
options.c: To ensure that all code is manually reviewed by external auditors.
options.d: To delay the deployment of software until all features are complete.

question: Which of the following best describes Threat Modeling?

options.a: A reactive process of patching bugs found after a system is hacked.
options.b: A structured way to identify and prioritize threats before a system is built.
options.c: A method to automate the deployment of web servers on cloud platforms.
options.d: A tool used to monitor network traffic for unauthorized access.

question: What is the primary function of a Data Flow Diagram (DFD) in threat modeling?

options.a: To visualize how data moves through a system and identify potential attack vectors.
options.b: To manage the scheduling of development team meetings and sprint cycles.
options.c: To automatically patch vulnerabilities found in third-party libraries.
options.d: To document the hardware specifications of the server environment.

question: Which tool is mentioned in the notes as helpful for creating visual threat modeling aids?

options.a: Metasploit.
options.b: Threat Dragon.
options.c: Nagios.
options.d: Zabbix.

question: What is the main difference between SAST and DAST in application security testing?

options.a: SAST analyzes code without running it; DAST tests the running application.
options.b: SAST requires network access; DAST works on physical hardware only.
options.c: SAST is for mobile apps; DAST is only for cloud environments.
options.d: SAST is a reactive method; DAST is a preventative design method.

question: Why is penetration testing described as more than just 'showing what is broken'?

options.a: Because it is primarily used to train new software developers.
options.b: Because it focuses on improving resilience before real attackers strike.
options.c: Because it ensures the software meets all aesthetic user requirements.
options.d: Because it guarantees that a system will never be breached again.

question: Which operating system environment is commonly associated with penetration testing tools like Metasploit?

options.a: Windows 11.
options.b: Kali Linux.
options.c: macOS.
options.d: Android OS.

question: When a SQL injection vulnerability is found, what is a recommended mitigation strategy?

options.a: Implementing parameterized queries and input validation.
options.b: Disabling the database entirely to prevent data leaks.
options.c: Switching the database to a public cloud provider.
options.d: Increasing the memory allocation of the database server.

question: What should be the outcome of a post-penetration testing debrief session?

options.a: A public announcement of all system vulnerabilities found.
options.b: A clear definition of next steps and remediation strategies.
options.c: The automatic termination of the development team.
options.d: The immediate purchase of more expensive firewall software.

question: Why is security considered a 'nice-to-have' by some, and why is this problematic?

options.a: It is not problematic; security should be optional in modern apps.
options.b: It is problematic because it often leads to late-stage vulnerabilities.
options.c: It is problematic because it increases the price of open-source software.
options.d: It is problematic because it makes software harder to install.

question: What is the primary purpose of asking 'What are we building?' during threat modeling?

options.a: To start the structured process of understanding system architecture.
options.b: To finalize the marketing budget for the software product.
options.c: To decide which programming language to use for the user interface.
options.d: To determine how many servers will be needed for hosting.

question: Which of these is a key component of an effective application security strategy?

options.a: A blend of threat modeling, SAST/DAST, and penetration testing.
options.b: Ignoring vulnerabilities until they are exploited by attackers.
options.c: Relying strictly on perimeter firewalls to block all malicious traffic.
options.d: Using only automated tools without any human security testing.

question: In the context of session management, what is a recommended mitigation for weak session tokens?

options.a: Hardcoding the session ID into the HTML source code.
options.b: Enforcing HTTPS and implementing strict timeout policies.
options.c: Using a simple numeric sequence for all generated tokens.
options.d: Storing all session tokens in an unencrypted plain text file.

question: How does proactive security differ from reactive security?

options.a: Proactive security focuses on prevention; reactive security focuses on response.
options.b: Proactive security is for cloud; reactive security is for mobile apps.
options.c: Proactive security is faster to implement; reactive security is cheaper.
options.d: Proactive security is handled by managers; reactive is by developers.

question: What is the consequence of treating security as an afterthought in development?

options.a: Faster deployment cycles and higher user satisfaction.
options.b: Vulnerabilities that are harder and more costly to fix later.
options.c: A decrease in the amount of documentation required.
options.d: Improved compatibility with older browser versions.

question: Why are visual aids used in threat modeling?

options.a: To help teams systematically identify threats in a complex system.
options.b: To make the project appear more professional to stakeholders.
options.c: To hide complex system details from junior team members.
options.d: To replace the need for security testing and code analysis.

question: Which of the following is an example of an application security testing practice?

options.a: Configuring the physical server rack for better airflow.
options.b: Conducting Dynamic Application Security Testing (DAST).
options.c: Updating the company website's marketing imagery.
options.d: Refactoring the logo design for better visibility.

question: What is the primary goal of a penetration test report?

options.a: To showcase the tester's skill level to the management.
options.b: To provide a structured list of findings and mitigation strategies.
options.c: To justify the budget for new office equipment.
options.d: To serve as a marketing tool for potential new customers.

question: Why is 'What could go wrong?' a critical question in threat modeling?

options.a: To identify potential vulnerabilities before the system is built.
options.b: To distract the development team from their deadlines.
options.c: To ensure that the application is profitable in the market.
options.d: To prevent the installation of unnecessary software tools.

question: Which methodology is often used for categorizing common web application vulnerabilities?

options.a: OWASP Top 10.
options.d: HTML5 Standards.

question: What role does automation play in modern security?

options.a: It enables continuous scanning through SAST and DAST.
options.b: It removes the need for any human security professionals.
options.c: It ensures that code never contains any bugs at all.
options.d: It replaces the need for a documented threat model.

question: What is the 'Big Picture' for application security?

options.a: Building software with proactive design and continuous testing.
options.b: Installing a powerful firewall to protect the perimeter.
options.c: Focusing only on the user interface for customer appeal.
options.d: Storing all application data in a secure offline vault.

question: Why is the software development lifecycle important in security?

options.a: Because security must be integrated at every phase of development.
options.b: Because it defines the hardware requirements for the servers.
options.c: Because it forces teams to work exclusively in local environments.
options.d: Because it limits the number of developers allowed on a project.

question: Which of these is a benefit of threat modeling?

options.a: Reducing risk by addressing threats early.
options.b: Increasing the number of features in a release.
options.c: Eliminating the need for software testing entirely.
options.d: Lowering the cost of cloud hosting services.

question: What are SAST and DAST examples of?

options.a: Application security testing methods.
options.b: Physical server cooling techniques.
options.c: Network cable management protocols.
options.d: Marketing strategies for mobile apps.

question: Why should teams debrief after security testing?

options.a: To ensure clear next steps for remediation.
options.b: To assign blame for the vulnerabilities found.
options.c: To delay the next software release cycle.
options.d: To celebrate the successful completion of the test.

question: How do DFDs help security teams?

options.a: By showing how data moves, exposing potential gaps.
options.b: By automatically fixing vulnerabilities in the code.
options.c: By speeding up the code compilation process.
options.d: By hiding sensitive database details from attackers.

question: What is a main focus of OWASP Top 10?

options.a: Highlighting critical web application vulnerabilities.
options.b: Managing employee benefits for cybersecurity firms.
options.c: Regulating the internet traffic of small businesses.
options.d: Providing free server hosting for web developers.

question: Why is application security an essential engineering process?

options.a: Because applications are prime targets for cyberattacks.
options.b: Because it allows developers to write code faster.
options.c: Because it is a requirement for hardware procurement.
options.d: Because it makes the software visually more appealing.

question: Which of these is a proactive security measure?

options.a: Threat modeling.
options.b: Reactive patching.
options.c: Hardware replacement.
options.d: Log file deletion.

question: Why should you use HTTPS?

options.a: To enforce stronger encryption in transit.
options.b: To speed up server responses for users.
options.c: To make the application look official.
options.d: To store data more efficiently in the database.

question: How does penetration testing improve resilience?

options.a: By identifying weaknesses before real attackers find them.
options.b: By removing the need for regular software updates.
options.c: By increasing the complexity of the user interface.
options.d: By reducing the need for threat modeling tools.

question: What is the first step of threat modeling?

options.a: Understanding what is being built.
options.b: Deploying the app to a production server.
options.c: Deleting all sensitive user information.
options.d: Running an automated scanner on the code.

question: Why are visual aids like DFDs effective?

options.a: They simplify understanding of complex system interactions.
options.b: They are mandated by international law for all apps.
options.c: They are required to get an application approved for app stores.
options.d: They eliminate the need for any technical documentation.

question: What do automated scanners like SAST provide?

options.a: Systematic checks of source code for security flaws.
options.b: Automatic physical security for the server room.
options.c: Fast loading times for web application users.

question: What is the purpose of threat modeling?

options.a: To systematically identify and prioritize threats.
options.b: To increase the cost of developing software apps.
options.c: To help managers track daily project tasks.

question: Why is testing necessary?

options.a: To ensure software behaves as expected securely.
options.b: To make sure the software is compatible with printers.
options.c: To avoid the need for any future security patches.
options.d: To make the software look professional for investors.

question: What is a vulnerability?

options.a: A weakness that can be exploited by an attacker.
options.b: A feature that increases the system's performance.
options.c: A piece of hardware that is difficult to repair.
options.d: A bug that prevents the code from compiling.

question: How can you minimize risk?

options.a: Through proactive design and continuous security testing.
options.b: By hiding the system's location from the internet.
options.c: By limiting the number of users on the system.
options.d: By skipping the software development lifecycle.

question: Why is Kali Linux popular?

options.a: Because it contains a suite of security testing tools.
options.b: Because it is required to play modern video games.
options.c: Because it is the only OS that supports web browsers.
options.d: Because it makes computer hardware run faster.

question: Which of these is a goal of security?

options.a: Protecting systems from malicious activity.
options.b: Reducing the battery life of mobile devices.
options.c: Increasing the complexity of user passwords.
options.d: Preventing software from ever being updated.

question: Why is threat modeling structured?

options.a: To ensure all threats are considered consistently.
options.b: To slow down the development of the application.
options.c: To require more paperwork for the management team.
options.d: To force developers to use specific languages.

question: What is a security test?

options.a: An attempt to find weaknesses in software security.
options.b: A process of checking for software bugs on websites.
options.c: A method to verify if a software product looks good.
options.d: An installation of new server hardware components.

question: Why is continuous testing important?

options.a: To catch vulnerabilities early as code changes.

options.b: A diagram of the physical server room layout.
options.c: A list of employee names working on the project.

question: What does 'shifting left' mean?

options.a: Moving security earlier in the development lifecycle.
options.b: Changing the programming language being used.
options.c: Moving the server to a different geographic region.
options.d: Prioritizing the user interface before the backend.

question: Why is penetration testing proactive?

options.a: Because it identifies flaws before they are exploited.

options.a: To test security vulnerabilities systematically.

options.a: To prevent injection-based attacks.
